topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon air hung thick and still, pressing against the windowpanes of the small apartment. Elias shuffled to the mailbox, a vague sense of aimlessness clinging to him like damp wool. He hadn't really been thinking about anything all day; the usual threads of his schedule had frayed into meaningless strands. Then, tucked beneath a stack of junk mail, he saw it—a card, pristine white, bearing no sender’s name, only a cheerful, looping script wishing someone a happy birthday. His fingers brushed the thick paper, and a strange, hollow ache settled in his chest. He turned it over, the paper cool beneath his fingertips, and found nothing but blankness where a message should have been. He stood there for a long moment, the silence of the hallway amplifying the frantic thrumming in his ears, before slowly tucking the card into his pocket, the weight of it feeling both insignificant and strangely heavy.

[story 2]

Clara stared at the card as if it were a foreign object dropped into her carefully ordered world. She had been walking aimlessly through the park, her mind a scattered collection of half-formed thoughts, when the sight of the unsolicited greeting caught her eye. It was unsigned, the paper thick and creamy, and the message, a simple 'Happy Birthday,' seemed utterly disconnected from the reality she was currently inhabiting. Her breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air. She ran a hand over her face, feeling a sudden, sharp vertigo, as if the ground beneath her had shifted. Who had done this? Why was it here? A knot tightened in her stomach, a restless energy bubbling beneath her skin. She didn't know whether to throw it away or examine it closer, her movements jerky and uncertain.

[story 3]

When Mark opened the metal box, the usual routine of sorting recyclables and junk mail felt alien. His eyes snagged on a card that seemed entirely out of place—a birthday card, perfectly creased, yet devoid of any identifying mark. He picked it up, the texture of the card surprisingly smooth against his palm. A peculiar blankness washed over him, a sense that the familiar contours of his day had suddenly dissolved into mist. He turned it over again, his gaze tracing the edges of the paper as if searching for a hidden instruction. A strange tremor ran through his hand. He stood motionless, the sound of traffic outside seeming distant and muffled, as if he were watching the world through thick, distorting glass. He didn't know what to do with the unexpected piece of paper, only that he couldn't seem to focus on anything else.
